message.content: Adversaries may exploit double file extensions to disguise the true nature of malicious files, making them appear benign. This technique involves appending a secondary, often dangerous, file extension (e.g., .exe, .lnk) after a seemingly harmless one (e.g., .txt, .jpg). The operating system or software may only display the first extension, misleading users into thinking the file is safe. Commonly, these files are used in spearphishing attacks, where a user is tricked into opening an attachment that appears to be a harmless document or image but is actually executable malware. This method can lead to initial access and subsequent execution of malicious code. Notable examples include the Bazar loader and campaigns by Mustang Panda. Mitigation strategies include configuring systems to display full file extensions, educating users about the risks of double extensions, and employing robust email filtering to detect and block suspicious attachments.
